original_abstract: 1,3-Disubstituted bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) are high-value motifs in medicinal chemistry, serving as robust, three-dimensional bioisosteres for para-substituted benzene rings. Despite their ability to improve the metabolic stability and solubility of drug candidates, their widespread adoption is hindered by the synthetic challenges associated with [1.1.1]propellane, a volatile and unstable precursor. Here, we introduce a series of stable BCP-thianthrenium reagents that serve as versatile platforms for O-, N-, and C-bicyclo[1.1.1]pentylation. These reagents are non-hygroscopic solids with high thermal stability, facilitating safe storage and handling. Leveraging the unique redox and stereoelectronic properties of the thianthrene scaffold, these salts undergo single-electron reduction to generate BCP radicals via selective mesolytic cleavage of the exocyclic C–S bond. This reactivity enables a range of photoredox-mediated transition-metal-catalyzed transformations, including the first reported synthesis of aryl bicyclo[1.1.1]pentyl ethers and broad-scope C–N and C–C cross-couplings. The method demonstrates exceptional functional group tolerance and is suitable for the late-stage diversification of complex pharmaceuticals, providing a practical and expansive toolkit for incorporating BCP motifs into bioactive molecules.
humanized_abstract: The syntheses and applications of stable salts of 1,3-Disubstituted bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) as three-dimensional bioisosteres for the para position of benzene have recently become of high importance for the drug industry in terms of increasing drug longevity, as well as increasing drug solubility. The main reason that there have not been a vast amount of studies done on these compounds is due to the difficulties of synthesizing [1.1.1]propellane. Here, we introduce stable salts of BCPs in the form of BCP-thianthrenium salts that can be utilized for the O-, N-, and C-bicyclo[1.1.1]pentylation of various different compounds and moieties through the use of photoredox and transition metal catalysis, while achieving high yield and functional group tolerance. The salts themselves exist as non-hygroscopic solids with increased thermal stability, when compared to other salts, and enable the compounds to be safely stored and handled, while undergoing single-electron reduction through mesolytic cleavage of the exocyclic C–S bond to form BCP radicals. The unique redox and stereoelectronic properties of thianthrene allow the salts to undergo a variety of different photoredox-mediated transition metal catalytic reactions. The scope and mechanism of these reactions have been explored in detail and include the first reported syntheses of aryl BCP ethers, as well as C–N and C–C cross coupling reactions with a broad scope and high functional group tolerance. These reactions enable the late stage diversification of complex pharmaceuticals and offer a versatile and expansive toolkit for the incorporation of BCPs into bioactive molecules.
